Summary: The paper proposes replacing the ReLU activation with its squared variant (ReLU^2) in the MLP blocks of GPT-2 small, and reports a perplexity reduction from 29.4 to 29.1 on WikiText-103. The method is described in a single line of code, and the empirical evaluation consists of one training run with a single random seed. No ablations, baselines beyond vanilla ReLU, theoretical analysis, or computational cost comparisons are provided. The contribution is positioned as a note rather than a full paper.
Strengths: The proposed modification is extremely simple to implement and reproduce, requiring only a one-line change in the MLP block.
The paper addresses a well-defined and practical question: whether a trivial activation swap can yield measurable language modeling improvements.
The writing is concise and the claim is unambiguous, making the scope of the contribution easy to assess.
Weaknesses: The empirical evidence is drastically insufficient: a single seed and a 0.3 PPL gap on one benchmark cannot establish statistical significance, especially given the known high variance of language model training.

There is no discussion of, or comparison to, prior work on squared/polynomial activations (e.g., Primer's Squared ReLU, So et al. 2021), which directly proposed and evaluated the same activation at much larger scale — the novelty claim is therefore unsubstantiated and the paper appears to rediscover known results without attribution.

No ablations are provided: no variation in model size, training budget, dataset, or initialization scheme; no analysis of activation statistics, gradient behavior, or training stability under the squared nonlinearity.
The paper omits all experimental details required for reproducibility: hyperparameters, optimizer, learning rate, training steps, tokenization, compute, and wall-clock cost are not reported.

No theoretical motivation or intuition is provided for why ReLU^2 should help; the method section is a single line and offers no analysis of the function's properties (non-Lipschitz gradients, unbounded activations, interaction with LayerNorm, etc.).
